pages.name: How Scaling Works (One Lookup, No Math)

text.content:
In Dark Souls, a weapon's power comes from two things: how well it scales with your stat, and how far you've upgraded it with titanite. Ashen models both with simple, consistent math — and a printed grid so nobody multiplies anything at the table.

Damage on a hit

Weapon dice  +  Scaling bonus  +  Upgrade (+N)

  • Weapon dice — printed on the weapon (e.g. a Greataxe rolls 1d12).
  • Scaling bonus — one number you read off the grid (next page) using your governing stat's modifier and the weapon's grade. This replaces the usual "add your ability modifier to damage" step — so you never add your stat twice.
  • Upgrade (+N) — if Andre has reinforced the weapon, add +N to both attack and damage. The sheet already includes this; you don't track it by hand.

To-hit is unchanged

Your attack roll is the normal d20 + proficiency + governing modifier + upgrade. Scaling only ever touches damage — just like a weapon's AR in the games. There is no "accuracy stat"; landing and avoiding hits is the dodge/block/parry minigame, not your numbers.

The one formula (for the curious)

Scaling bonus = floor( governing modifier × grade factor ), where the factors are S 2.0 / A 1.5 / B 1.0 / C 0.75 / D 0.5 / E 0.25. Grade B is exactly normal 5e (your full modifier). Higher grades reward stat investment; lower grades barely care who swings the weapon. You never need this formula at the table — just read the grid.

Find your weapon's grade (left column) and your governing stat modifier (top). That cell is the flat damage you add to every hit. Each weapon card also reprints its own row, so pickups need no chart.

Grade+0+1+2+3+4+5+6
S (×2.0)024681012
A (×1.5)0134679
B (×1.0)0123456
C (×0.75)0012334
D (×0.5)0011223
E (×0.25)0000111

Example: a Greataxe is Strength, Grade A. With a +4 Strength modifier you add 6: a hit deals 1d12 + 6 (+ any upgrade). Pour points into Strength and that climbs fast — an S-grade weapon at +5 would add 10.

Governing stat by weapon family

  • Strength — greataxes, maces, most heavy/blunt weapons, the longsword.
  • Dexterity — daggers, scimitars, thrusting swords, spears, bows & crossbows.
  • Intelligence — staves (sorceries). Faith — talismans (miracles). Pyromancy flames use the higher of the two.

Each weapon scales off a single stat in Ashen (the wiki's primary one) to keep the lookup to one cell. A few weapons split scaling in the source game; we use the dominant stat.

At Firelink, Andre of Astora reinforces weapons with titanite recovered from the High Wall. Upgrading is flat, predictable, and capped for a one-shot so the numbers stay sane against 75–150 HP bosses.

What an upgrade does

Each +1 adds +1 to attack and +1 to damage. The reachable cap this session is +3. (Think of a +3 weapon like a +3 magic weapon in 5e — it's a real power spike, which is why titanite is scarce.)

Cost

Each +1 step costs 1 Titanite Shard + 200 souls at Andre's forge. A Titanite Chunk substitutes for the shards of a full jump to +3.

Reinforce toCost (cumulative)Effect (cumulative)
+11 Titanite Shard + 200 souls+1 attack / +1 damage
+22 Titanite Shards + 400 souls+2 attack / +2 damage
+33 Titanite Shards (or 1 Titanite Chunk) + 600 souls+3 attack / +3 damage

Titanite Shards and Chunks drop from elites, bosses, and the loot tables. A Chunk is worth roughly three Shards and jumps a weapon straight toward +3.

Catalysts upgrade too

Andre tempers staves, talismans, and the pyromancy flame the same way; on a catalyst, each +1 adds +1 to spell attack rolls and +1 to spell damage. Catalysts also scale: read the grid using the catalyst's grade and your Intelligence or Faith modifier, and add that to a spell's damage or healing.

DM note

To apply an upgrade in Foundry, bump the weapon's upgrade flag and add +N to the activity's attack & damage bonus (or just say it out loud and add the flat number — the math is identical). The weapon card's Upgrade line tracks the current level.
